title: Inheritance basics
instruction:
`class Child : public Parent` inherits all public members.

The derived class constructor must call the base class constructor:
```cpp
Dog(string name) : Animal(name) {}  // : calls base constructor
```

Run this.
successMessage: Animal constructor ran first, then Dog's. Rex inherited eat() from Animal without rewriting it. protected means derived classes can access the member; private would hide it even from derived classes.

title: virtual — enable polymorphism
instruction:
Without `virtual`, the BASE class version is always called even on derived objects.
With `virtual`, the DERIVED version is called based on the actual object type:

```cpp
virtual void speak() { cout << "..." << endl; }  // base
void speak() override { cout << "Woof!" << endl; }  // derived
```

`override` is optional but essential — it makes typos compile errors.
successMessage: makeSpeak takes Animal& but calls the right speak() — Dog's for Rex, Cat's for Whiskers. This is virtual dispatch. Without virtual, all three would print '...' — the base version.

title: Pure virtual — abstract classes
instruction:
A **pure virtual** function has no implementation in the base class:

```cpp
virtual double area() = 0;  // = 0 makes it pure virtual
```

A class with any pure virtual function is **abstract** — you cannot instantiate it directly. Derived classes MUST implement all pure virtuals.
successMessage: Abstract classes enforce a contract — every Shape subclass MUST implement area() and perimeter(). Shape itself can't be instantiated — just a blueprint.

title: Virtual destructor — essential!
instruction:
If you delete a derived object through a base pointer without a virtual destructor, the derived destructor never runs — **memory leak!**

Rule: **If a class has virtual functions, give it a virtual destructor.**
successMessage: Both destructors ran — Derived first, then Base. Without virtual ~Base(), only Base::~Base() would run — the expensive resource would never be freed. Virtual destructor is non-optional for polymorphic base classes.

title: dynamic_cast — safe downcasting
instruction:
`dynamic_cast` safely converts a base pointer/reference to a derived type:

- Returns `nullptr` if the cast fails (for pointers)
- Throws `bad_cast` if it fails (for references)

Requires virtual functions (vtable) to work.
successMessage: dynamic_cast returns nullptr for the wrong type — safe to check. This is how game engines handle entity-specific interactions: 'is this entity a Player? Let it interact with the upgrade.'

title: Build a shapes calculator
instruction:
No starter code.

Build an abstract class hierarchy:
- `abstract class Shape` — pure virtual `area()`, `perimeter()`, `name()` methods; virtual `describe()` that prints all three
- `class Circle(radius)`, `class Rectangle(w, h)`, `class Triangle(a, b, c)` — all concrete
- Triangle area: Heron's formula — `s=(a+b+c)/2; area=sqrt(s*(s-a)*(s-b)*(s-c))`

Create a vector of `Shape*`, call describe() on each, total the areas.
successMessage: Abstract class, three concrete shapes, polymorphic dispatch. Virtual functions call the right area() for each shape. This is the foundation of every game engine, rendering system, and UI framework. Chapter 14 done!
